displayTitle: 2026-04-30 - Transport and Infrastructure Committee - Inquiry into Ports and the Maritime Sector
topic: Inquiry into Ports and the Maritime Sector
description: 30 April 2026 | Transport and Infrastructure Committee Inquiry into Ports and the Maritime Sector A.P. Moller Tony Mildon, Cold Chain Sales Director, Oceania Area
descriptionSummary: Inquiry into Ports and the Maritime Sector. A.P. Moller. Tony Mildon, Cold Chain Sales Director, Oceania Area

chunks.text: Make some introductory comments for a few minutes then that's great, and then we will ask lots and lots of questions. Just so you know, I don't know if you've followed where we've got to, but we're getting close to the end of hearing from everyone that we can hear from. Probably like, yeah, I think we're trying to get MSC in here as well.

We've been to seaport, have ports in here, we've had customers in here, we've land-based transport providers, we've had unions. So we've still got a few to go, but we're trying to get our coastal shipping. We're trying to get just a picture overall that we can then make some judgments on, but it's very important for us to have the major shipping lines in here too.

So we're very grateful to you here. Bye. Thank you, and thank you for the opportunity to be here. I think that just as an open statement, certainly from Maersk, when we look at New Zealand and the importance of trade for New Zealand and the fact that we're so far away from our markets, very much, we look at a very long, skinny supply chain.

Unlike other trades in the world where we have vessels that go around onto a number of ports and a number of countries, there is the ability to mix and match cargo so that you're driving good utilization. When we come to New Zealand, it's completely reliant on the trade into New Zealand and out of New Zealand to fill the vessel and drive the utilization both ways.

So what's very important for us is how those vessels work the coast, how they make sure that they are spending as least time as possible on the coast, and are we doing the best for the New Zealand exporter and importer in terms of what the service that we're providing. So what you'll see from our submission, there were four pillars around that that we believe that were important to drive this.

I'm happy to talk to them individually, or we can do- Well, I think if you want to highlight some key points, that would be very useful So the key pillars that we see in terms of if we're going to position ourselves in New Zealand for the growth that we're looking to drive and also attack cost and efficiencies.

When we look at New Zealand right now and you see there's roughly around about nine ports, of which three are significant, and then there's a lot of other ports. And you see when you look at driving ocean capacity around those ports, what you do is you actually chew up important transit time.

So, a network can spend seven to 10 days on the New Zealand coast. So that means from the first port it loads to the last port it loads, if you're on the first port, your transit to your key market is plus seven days than the last port. So there's some inequities on the time that the ship's been on the water.

The model that we have run in the past, what we run now, and is very successful is the hub and spoke model. So that's really the first bit that we'll talk to. What that really means in terms of a New Zealand operation is you focus your major network. So for us, it's the Southern Star, which is our network that goes up to Tangin Palapas in Malaysia.

So when you talk about a network, you're meaning a ship. Voyager ships. Yeah. Yeah. So this network is called the Southern Star network. It currently comes down, it runs north-south through the coast. It then goes up to Tangin Palapas, which is our major hub. And from there, we touch every service around the world. So from there, you'll go anywhere from Northeast Asia, Southeast Asia, South Asia, Africa, Middle East, Europe.

That's the- So essentially, you're arriving Auckland. Yeah. Going down the coast. Yeah. Then departing. Departing Port Chalmers. Port Chalmers. Yeah. And going to? Tangin Palapas in Malaysia. Malaysia, right, okay. So then if you look at what the length of time that spends on the coast and then you sort of say, "Okay, well, how could this be better?" So our view on life is we would look to the New Zealanders establish two hubs.

So you'd have one in the North Island and one in the South Island, and then you would feed those hubs either by an improved coastal network, or you would look at the land-based infrastructure to support that, and the obvious one being rail. So that would be Tauranga, Lyttelton. I don't like putting names on ports, but you'd have to pick in the North Island, Tauranga would be a strong contender. South Island would be a question of how you'd look at that.

Then to support that, you need the coastal network that's going to support that. In the South Island, you could argue that eastern seaboard, that could be rail, right? But if you look at the North Island, where you've got places like Nelson and Napier, which well, Nelson in the South Island, but Napier in the North Island, which aren't as well-serviced by rail, then you would need to have a coastal network. To support that, we would need some changes in the legislation just around the coastal network because the legislation sort of precludes us

operating efficiently around the coast, and that's the same for any ocean carrier. The second leg of the discussion is the operational performance of some of the container terminals. We need to really nail down how we get some improvements in that space.

So if you look at the major New Zealand ports, they're all in the bottom quartile according to World Bank ratings on efficiency and how they work. Mm. There is some work that's been done in Tauranga which will help if it comes through, but it doesn't provide the full answer.

We need to look at how the cargo's flowing through those terminals and how they're operating. We believe that- Can you just tell us what your measure of efficiency is? Because we've had quite a number of different measures of efficiency which have been given to us that don't all marry up- Yes... from different parties. So the World Bank publishes a-The measure of efficiency, I don't have that particular number now, but it's a recognized measure. And that's where we're taking our information from and our comparison from. So we're not looking at

Australia, New Zealand ports or anything. We're looking at comparable-sized ports around the world. Oh, so you're not comparing one of our ports with Singapore or- No... someplace. Yeah. No. It's comparable- Comparable sized ports... comparable sized ports and operations. The other part of the pillar that we've looked at, and I touched on it earlier, is just what are we doing with the inland infrastructure to support the ships when, and the terminals when they're alongside.

What's our ability to build inland hubs so that we can stage cargo in that takes the pressure off the terminals when the ships are in, and it allows the ship to come in and the terminal will work very efficiently. And the last one is really just around this, developing this sustainable and safe skill.

We're very much focused on the development of people, development of skill, and safety, and safety culture around the operations. Okay. Questions time. Yep. Right. Okay. I'm going to give Simon the first crack, and then I'm going to put the next three. But- Okay Hang on. No, seriously, I'll take it one then after Simon Wego.

Thank you, Mr. Chair. So a couple of things. You talked about efficiency of container handling. We saw Port of Auckland, with the best of intentions, attempt an automation project. It hasn't succeeded in the way they wanted, but we've seen automation being delivered in other ports around Asia Pacific to a greater or lesser extent.

What do you think this committee should be looking at in terms of how we get the best out of automation? Is it a stage thing? Is it a scale thing? Is it a pick a technology thing? What are the things we should be thinking about? So when we look at it, the best way to develop that is to use proven known technologies.

And it would be fair to say that if you look at where we've been successful in the past and where we've developed terminals, it's by leveraging what we learned around the world and what works in the local environment. I don't think that there is one answer. It's a case of looking at the model that's been run.

Is the port a transshipment port or a hub? Is the port a feeder port? How much do you need to invest in that life? But really the focus needs to be on those hub ports. Yeah. So there is no easy answer, but it's a case of leveraging off experience. Yeah. Okay. And just in terms of the land-based infrastructure or inland port infrastructure, how close does an inland port need to be to the water for it to be operationally, functionally effective?

And what kind of connections are necessary? Are we talking about direct rail into right under the crane, or are we talking a road freight route? How should we optimize that? So our view very much on that one, and this is partly around an emissions discussion as well, is the inland terminal needs to have rail infrastructure in, and it needs to be serviced by rail. Part of one of the drivers around rail, and when you look at the development that we have done in Raukura and then the connection to the Raukura Rail Siding is the fact

that you can fully utilize the capacity of a container on a rail wagon, which you can't use on a road wagon. So you need that scale. The terminal needs to have a strong rail marshaling area, so it doesn't need to go direct to crane. But the principle of an inland hub is that you start collecting cargo 10 days out before the ship arrives, but you don't want 10 days'

worth of vessel cargo on the terminal. So that allows the terminal then to call the cargo in after they've worked the previous ship. Mm. So an inland hub can be anything between four to five hours away- Right... from the terminal itself. Where you drive true benefit is when you place that inland hub close to the manufacturer or the processing of the product.

Interesting. So then you take away the road haul, and you then provide the customer a viable alternative to attack their transport cost, but then you collect it and use rail. Very helpful insights. Thank you very much. So an example of that might be if you've got a railway line servicing, I'm going to use Fonterra as a major customer. You've got a railway line pretty much directly servicing a major plant.

Yep. The railway then can take you straight up to- Mm... even if it's three hours, whatever it might be, can still take you straight up to your marshalling yards in your inland port, and then straight onto the ship, straight up to the- It's a bit more than that, to be honest. A bit more than that, to be fair, from our view, because if you look at major plants like Fonterra plants that are all rail-enabled- Mm... that's 100%, the container will rail in and rail out.

That's how it works. Yeah. If you look at a lot of the meat processing plants, they have either decommissioned their siding, or they don't have a siding. So that will never work for them. So from a line perspective, and you think about if you put a rail siding in the middle of, again, I'll use the Waikato as an example.

You've got probably withinThree to four hours on a truck, you've got at least 10 parts- Mm-hmm... all individual, many different parts. So what you do is you then put the empty container in the middle of them. So the truck for the customers just took that to Hamilton, back out to Te Kuiti, for example, and then they bring it full back to Hamilton or Raurimu, and then it goes on rail into Tauranga or Auckland from there.

Yeah. Can you just talk us through the hub and spoke bit? So at the moment, your ships, just take us through, because it sounds as though they're docking at multiple different ports in New Zealand. Yes. You're telling us effectively that that's not ideal.

You'd rather dock at less of them, but do more work at fewer of them, and then have the rest serviced by either coastal shipping or rail or whatever else it might be. Yeah. So there's a version of that. If you look at, for Maersk today, we run essentially five different networks through New Zealand.

Okay. So we have a network that goes to Tangier and Pelorus, which is our main workhorse. So that's our big network. So that's the Southern Star. Sorry, so where did it go? So that's the one that currently goes Auckland, Napier, Littleton, Port Chalmers, Tangier, and Pelorus. Auckland, Napier. Yeah. So it's currently calling Napier as well for the minute for the apples.

Then if you look at another service we run, which is a similar vessel, that's called the Northern Star. Yeah. And that calls Napier, Nelson, Timaru, Tauranga, and that goes up to North Asia. So that goes up into China. Yeah. And so it's more of a direct service. The third network we run is a direct service to the East Coast of North America, and that goes Port Chalmers, Tauranga out.

Then we have shared networks. We have one up to the West Coast, which only calls Tauranga. And then we have another shared network which goes direct into Japan, which calls five ports in New Zealand. Our view on life is if we looked at, say, the Southern Star became our main, for want of a better terminology, hub and spoke service, is that you would then use, that would come down to Tauranga, Littleton, for the sake of this conversation, and go to Malaysia.

So it spends less time on the coast, means you get faster transits for those key markets up to Pelorus and beyond. Then you use the other networks to feed a cargo. So you would still definitely need an ocean feeder for somewhere like Nelson, which is land-based trade.

You'd definitely need the what? You'd definitely need a feeder service and a coastal network service. So we are talking a coastal shipping service, because that's one of the things is that-... you get from the coastal shipping side is you're competing on going to all those different places. Yeah. You're competing with coastal shipping. Your price, your economics is much stronger than theirs is.

But what you're saying is actually, from your perspective, what you're doing at the moment is not optimal. No. I think it's important for us to differentiate between coastal and feeder. Mm. So what we're actually operating is a feeder network because we're only taking deep sea cargo. Mm. So we're focusing with the customer to put our container into their port so that they can load it, and then we're feeding it into our mainline network.

So for example, Nelson is a classic example. We have one of our services, Northern Star, it'll come down into Nelson from Asia. It's a smaller ship, so it can work Nelson easier. And we'll go in there, and we'll position our empties in there for that market. Now, the apples that go from Nelson go all over the world.

Mm. So that will then pick up those apples and then take them to Tauranga or to Port Chalmers to connect to the different vessels, whether they're going to the East Coast, North America, whether they're going to Europe, wherever they're going. So what we're talking about here is a feeder operation. We're not out there competing in the coastal market.

We're just putting our containers that we're going to load and around. Dan? Thanks, Tony. Just continuing on the hub and spokes model, you mentioned in your submission around Section 198. Yeah. Just want to get a clear understanding of, so you have your five child routes.

Yeah. I'm guessing that Section 198 applies to all five. Is that correct? Yes. In an ideal world, are you telling us that you think if we can change 198 or get rid of it, then what does that mean for Maersk in terms of the five? That you collapse that to two or three?

Five. Yeah. So on the principle that four of the networks are offering really for us key direct markets as well. So the direct service to the East Coast, North America, the direct service to the West Coast, the direct service to North Asia. From the way our market share is working now, that will remain. The amendment to the act that we see that would drive a better cost efficiency, and that is probably the one area, is the ability to double call a port and a rotation.
To double. Can you explain that for us? Yes. So for example, on

the rotation right now, a deep sea ocean-going vessel can only call a continuous voyage. Mm. So if we say, for example, from a business customer need-We say that we're going to come down, we're going to go to Christchurch, we're going to go to Timaru to pick cargo up in Timaru, then we're going to go back to Christchurch- Got you... to load.

We're not allowed to do that because that's not continuous. Yeah. That's 198, isn't it? Yeah. Yeah. Yes. So that's the reference to the act there, that change, it wouldn't see fewer services. It would just allow us to be more efficient. With the vessels, you've got the balance of flows of weight, of draft and- Yeah... imports, so you can only go in with certain drafts into so many ports, so you've got to be very careful about what you load.

Because New Zealand, and if you look at it, we're an export dominant country, and a very large part of our business is, and the business of all ocean carriers, is your ability to put empty containers around the coast. Yeah. So sometimes it works better if you just get a double one. And is it your view that Section 198's kind of out of step with other markets? How does it...

If we moved- Yeah... away from that or amended it, would it be bringing us more into line with other markets? I think that different countries have very much different rules around what you can do on the coast. The US have got the Jones Act. Australia have got their legislation as well.

I think that the challenge that we have in New Zealand is just the distance and the range of options and choice. Really, if you look at it from each of the networks, you get one call a week, and if you can't get that right, then you're in trouble. Versus you go to some of these wider international markets, there's a vessel every second day.

Okay, thank you. Can I take on that? Keep going. Go on. Yeah. What is the impetus behind that legislation, no double? Yeah. That it needs continuous. What problem was that trying to solve, and how can we make those changes while still not opening up the original?

I don't know. Was it a legacy of something that no one could- Yeah, it's a legacy. It's been there for as long as I've sort of been kicking around. Mm. So it is very much a legacy legislation. I think that there will be other parties that have very, very strong views.

Right. Because it was formed from something. Yeah. Yeah. So going back to your, you haven't spoke at the moment, and so you've got the five different networks, but what I'm hearing is that they are not the optimal arrangement. Clearly, you want the 198 so that you can actually do something where you're going to the same port twice.

Yeah. At the very least. But if you had your optimal arrangement, what would it look like? So it wouldn't be a lot different than what you see today. Yeah. In terms of the, I don't see fewer vessels come, because we will maintain at least four of those strengths because they provide us with direct products into different parts of the market, which is very important for us.

Different parts of the world, yeah. Yep. But the ability to use some of those smaller vessels to move cargo, or to feed up cargo around the coast, both import and export, is the efficiency or the gain that would return efficiencies back to the New Zealand market.

And do you still need excuse me, a range of different sizes of vessels to service New Zealand, presumably because the destination markets are also a different scale for us in terms of the amount of product going to them? Yeah. So it's a combination of markets. It's also a combination of, if we're serving coastal ports, what size vessel can that port handle?

Mm. What's the total length it can handle? What's the total draft it can handle? So there, if you look at our workhorse service, the Southern Star, it's certainly limited to ports it can call into New Zealand. So if you're looking, because we've had quite a bit of discussion about how many- Right... how many TEU any given port can take. So if you're looking a few years down the track, we understand, I think, the vast majority now of new vessels, and with the knowledge of the vessels going to be around a while, the vast majority of new vessels are

going to be 8,000 TEU plus. So you're always going to get a range of vessels. Yeah. If you look at what we're building right now, we're building in the 8,000 to 16,000 TEU range. Yeah. We've got a fleet of the 23, 24,000 TEU range, but the model that we are running globally is quite different these days, and it's quite different from other lines.

So when we use the big vessels, 24,000 TEU vessels, they're on a network called the Gemini. So they're only going to call about five or six ports, or eight ports in the world. Yeah. And if you are a New Zealand exporter today, you'd use the Southern Star. You'd go up to Malaysia to our transit port.

You'd then get on one of the 24,000 TEU vessels, and you'll go up to Rotterdam, and we'll put it all in Rotterdam. And then in Rotterdam, we're using the 8,000 to 16,000 TEU vessels to essentially run a bus service. So you'll be dropped in Rotterdam, and using the technology and the track, we can guarantee connections- Yeah... while the two vessels are alongside working.

Mm. And making the most of the technology that we have. And then it'll go to Hamburg, it'll go to Copenhagen, or go to wherever that end destination is. So when you started, you said that basically we want two core ports, one in the north, one in the south. Yep. I'm trying to reconcile that with Having a range of different size vessels that are also calling at the smaller ports as well. So the two core ports would, for example, be Tauranga and Dalton. Then you would use our North American service that would pick up cargo from there, and imports

would drop the imports into Nelson that needed to be in Nelson, put the empties into Nelson, load the freight up, then take it to another port to go back to Palapis. So it's a case of still using our existing networks, but it's using them more efficiently. So what needs to change at those ports, those big hub ports?

So they need to improve their efficiency and their rating. They need to improve their crane moves. They need to improve the overall throughput because essentially what we're going to do, we're not actually going to take volume away from the ports around, but we are going to focus volume on those two big ports. So they need to be able to step up and handle it.

Okay. Tony's got another question about- It's a question, which is about cabotage. Mm-hmm. And I've taken the point about the 198 and the reference to continuous journey. The other sort of aspect to that is that the cargo needs to be incidental to what is kind of on board. So we've heard a few views, different views around that- Yep... particular aspect.

What is your sort of acknowledgement or thinking in that particular space? In terms of- Around the incidental. In terms of the, sorry the- The inclusion of cargo, so for coastal- Yep... purposes, it needs to be incidental to the loading on the vessel. Yeah. There's been some suggestion, for example, that perhaps that word shouldn't exist, but it should be more qualified in terms of quantum, those sorts of things.

So just interested in your thoughts. So I'm not particularly right over that one. But I think very much that for us it's about the feeder cargo for our network. Mm-hmm. We're focused on the deep sea cargo. We're not focused on the coastal cargo. Okay. But the fact that you are sort of on a journey- Yep... around the place, still domestic cargo is a key part of or maybe not a key part, is a part of what you carry?

Yeah. It's certainly not a key part of what we carry. What we have to balance up with is the capacity on the vessel for our deep sea cargo, so we can't load, we won't load domestic cargo at the expense of loading the deep sea box. We also need to be very aware and cognizant of the impact to our ability to place empties into markets. I think it's fair to say, it is fair to say that we place a lot of empties around from Auckland and Tauranga down south. So if there is ability to load some cargo into that, but it's not our core, it's just

the ability to move a, put a box down in the South Island to load for export. Wow. So if you were able to do, I mean, you can kind of see that in terms of sovereign resilience and all that sort of stuff you need, every nation needs to be able to have internal transport networks.

Yes. And you essentially being able to feed your own deep sea product around the country is what you're asking-... for the ability to do, not get involved in otherwise other domestic cabotage. Yeah. But what would prevent you from stifling other ability to be able to grow coastal shipping and cabotage?

Or not cabota- and not rely on cabotage. So our focus is for ownership to go- Mm-hmm... to and from New Zealand. When we operate around the coast, we are quite often at capacities that we can't load domestic cargo. Mm. So when we look at, when certainly when the season's running hot like right now, when we sail somewhere like from Napier to join another vessel or when we sail south, we're full.

We're hitting our dead weight, we're hitting our numbers because it's carrying cargo that's going to go up to Tauranga, be discharged, and then go deep sea, and then that's the space that we load into from Tauranga off to other ports. Mm-hmm. So it's fair to say that it's not something that, it's fair to say that it's not something that we don't look at, but we have to look after our deep sea customers first.

First, yeah. Can I just follow up on that? And I appreciate your answers, and I appreciate that you're a business. But if you're saying that your focus is on deep sea product and not the domestic stuff, does your pricing mechanisms for domestic cargo align with that approach?

So it's not sort of an incentivization in terms of cost to carry domestic product or cargo in terms of the operations. So our approach on pricing is it's not a target to lift. So we're not going to go and price into pure coastal cargo because it's not a priority for us.

If we have a customer that has a need, comes to us and says, "Look, you're doing all my deep sea, can you move something southbound?" We will look at it if it suits our needs for putting empty equipment down there. If it doesn't suit our needs for putting empty equipment, so for example, we won't look at coastal cargo south, north.Because we're container deficit in the South Island, so we will only focus on the deep sea cargo.

Okay. Helpful. Can I just follow that up? So sending lots of empties down south. Yeah. I'm assuming that is because there is more product being created down south which needs exporting than there is relative to north. Yeah. Correct? Yeah. So the nature of New Zealand is 75% of the import market goes to the upper North Island through Tauranga- Yeah... Northland. Probably 20% will go in through Lyttelton, and then 5% through other areas.

The nature of our inbound market is heavily leveraged into dry containers. So your 40-foot standard containers because we're buying- That's the nature of the import market. Yeah, it's the nature of the market. So, you've got your outdoor furniture, your barbecues, your TVs, all those wonderful things that seem to be on special every week. Your outbound market, when you come down to the South Island, is heavily leveraged around food, meat, dairy, seafood. So that's a reefer export market.

So everything that we can bring into New Zealand, when we're doing our import market plan, we have to balance on the dry cargo coming in, but we also have to bring empty reefers in. And the empty reefers come from different places, to feed into New Zealand so that we have the market. If you look at the South Island, South Island is very strong in both refrigerated cargo, but also with the dairy industry and dry containers as well.

So we're forever moving containers around to where the demand is for load. So that's a very important part of the balancing planning that we do. So 75% of the imports come into North Island? Yeah. Question for Tracy here. What's the volume- What's the split... the volumes you're seeing in terms of the export side of things- Right... island to, north versus south?

In terms of rough percentages, these days, it'll probably be about 55% North Island, 45% South Island. Roughly. And that 75% you said in the first place, that was the upper North Island. Yes. What about the lower North Island? That's on top of that as well.

To be fair now, most of the stuff that goes into, say, potentially over Tauranga is covering- It's covering the whole North Island. Yeah. It's pretty much covering the North Island. So outbound's 55/45- Yeah... North Island, South Island. Yeah. So it's very useful numbers. And your pattern, do you think will be influenced by other shipping lines that service New Zealand?

So- Those numbers, you think will be broadly reflective of- Broadly reflective. Yeah. Yeah. Okay. So the bit about in and out, because we export a lot of refrigerated goods, so reefers. Yeah. So we're getting a huge chunk of our container movement is going to be reefers going out, then coming back empty.

And dry goods are largely the reverse. Coming in full and going back out empty. Yeah. So that can't do a whole lot. There's not much we can do about it, but they can't do a whole lot for the cost of servicing New Zealand. Yeah. I can say from a Maersk position, because of our position with certainly one key customer, in terms of our dry boxes, we're pretty good- Oh, yeah... at using our dry boxes out.

But the empty boxes, we need to bring in. So it's a lot of milk powder that goes out of New Zealand. Yeah. So we're quite well. So dry boxes aren't too bad. It's the reefers. It's the reefers that we need to bring in. Yeah. The dry boxes all come into the North Island for the consumables, and then we need to clean them up and move them down the line.

So dry box is reasonably balanced. Ish. Yes. Yeah. Yeah. Okay. Somebody else got a question while I'm contemplating my exterior of brown twit. Well, okay, back us well. On your improved performance of container terminals, I've now found the efficiency measure you're using is crane moves per hour.

Yeah. The bit that we've also got is the number of cranes you actually apply to any given vessel. In other words, how quickly you get the vessel loaded- Yeah... and unloaded, because obviously if you apply more cranes, they're both the same level of cranes per hour or movements per hour, you get it out twice as quickly. So is that the best model for you? Because I think what we heard from Auckland, and I think it was Auckland in particular, was that efficiency's not bad for crane moves per hour, but they only apply one of them

as opposed to they should maybe apply two. Yeah. That was the kind of thing that came through, I think. Yeah. Yeah. Well, I think, certainly the Ports of Auckland have more than one crane on their terminal. Mm-hmm. So it's your ability to lean into those cranes and use them. You will have some challenges around the length of the vessel and how many cranes you can put to a vessel.

Yes, that's right. Yeah. But we sort of view that when we look at the terminal efficiency, there's gains to be made by leveraging the learnings from some of these international terminals and the way they operate. Mm-hmm. We tend to see that the, in all fairness, that all the measures and numbers they use are quite internal or local and not really benchmarking against similar port sizes around the world.

So what are the key things that we do? I mean, you've made a suggestion about granting concessions for operations to experienced operators, which is a different model to what we've got here. But what are the key changes you would see in the ports themselves to make them more efficient? And while I'm asking a question about port, the one thing that does come through time and time again is the centrality of Tauranga.

Yeah. And that they're a little bit short of wharf space. I think that the conversation going around Tauranga and the wharf space there is a very important one, and one that needs to be settled. The concept for Stella Wharf. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. That's only part of the story, though. Yeah. You don't solve or hit the new heights just by making things bigger, because then potentially you make the problem bigger.

Yeah. So unless there's the investment to go along with it, and investment needs to be in hardware, cranes, straddles. The investment also needs to be around software and systems and processes that they're using. The investment also needs to be in some of these inland ports to stage cargo to come in.

So the investment is multi-layered. We firmly believe that international operators have a role to play there and an opportunity there to come in and achieve some of those efficiencies. You mean international operators on the port itself? Yep. Yeah.
Yes. Okay.

Just one of the things, the other question I was going to ask was just the attractiveness of New Zealand as a market to Maersk and to other shipping operators.
